entities.description: A conceptual grouping of two distinct anonymous Middle English prose romances copied together by one hand. Thebes abridges Lydgate's Siege of Thebes; Troy abridges Lydgate's Troy Book. This entity does not merge the two works or stand for the physical codex.
entities.label: Anonymous prose Sege of Thebes and prose Sege of Troye

rights.basis: The anonymous texts and their Lydgate sources are medieval. This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editorial apparatus, or photographs.
rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century Middle English wording

entities.description: The immediate printed source of CME00076. Brie's article introduces the two romances on pp. 40–46, prints Thebes on pp. 47–52 and 269–272, and prints Troy on pp. 273–285. Brie credits Fritz Jung for the transcript, expands manuscript abbreviations in italics, and supplies selected Lydgate parallels in footnotes.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1913; U-M selected it for a collection asserted No Copyright–United States, and Internet Archive marks the scanned volume NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT.

entities.description: The material intermediary from which Brie printed the two prose romances. Brie explicitly thanks Dr. Fritz Jung of Pforzheim for the transcript. entities.description: A fifteenth-century English paper codex of 34 leaves with three later endleaves at each end. Part 1, leaves 1–24, contains the two prose romances in one hybrida hand; part 2 contains a Gower extract and later verse. The current catalog infers Thebes at fols. 1r–10v and Troy at fols. 11r–24v.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. D. 82

physicalDescription.extent: iii later endleaves + 34 manuscript leaves + iii later endleaves
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex in a non-contemporary binding repaired in 1986
notes: Part 1 comprises three eight-leaf quarto quires and has 29 ruled and written lines in one hybrida hand.
Thebes is cataloged at inferred fols. 1r–10v; Troy at inferred fols. 11r–24v.
Part 2, fols. 25–34, contains a Confessio Amantis extract and later verse and is outside the CME00076 work boundary but inside the complete physical object.
The current catalog says part 1 has no foliation, while Late Medieval English Scribes reports modern pencil foliation 1–34.

reviewNotes: CME00076 is an editorial composite of two distinct medieval works, not one medieval romance. Part-level scopes prevent the Thebes and Troy subjects, sources, and loci from being conflated.
The XML omits Brie's German source study on pp. 40–46 but retains the transcription statement and both complete Middle English text runs: Thebes on pp. 47–52 and 269–272, and Troy on pp. 273–285.
The unnumbered PB element between printed pp. 52 and 270 represents p. 269, the opening page of the second journal installment.

Griffin printed The Sege of Troye in 1907, six years before Brie described both prose romances as previously unknown and their texts as first reproduced. Griffin is therefore a prior independent edition, not Brie's source and not an ancestor of the CME encoding.

Griffin proposed an unidentified French intermediary for Troy. Brie later demonstrated dependence on Lydgate's Troy Book, and the current METS Troy Book introduction identifies the prose Sege of Troy as an early-to-mid-fifteenth-century derivative of Lydgate.

The Bodleian's 2025 description calls the part-1 loci inferred and says that part has no foliation; Late Medieval English Scribes instead reports modern pencil foliation 1–34. The work ranges are usable institutional locators but should not be represented as an unproblematic medieval foliation.

The complete-source rule applies to the physical manuscript, not merely to the 24 leaves containing the corpus item. A future complete surrogate should retain adjacent Gower material, later verse, blanks, endleaves, pastedowns, covers or boards, spine, and edges when offered.

lineage.summary: CME00076 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread encoding of the Middle English text on Friedrich Brie's 1913 journal pages 47–52 and 269–285. Brie printed two distinct anonymous prose romances from one working transcript made by Fritz Jung from Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. D. 82: The Sege of Thebes, now cataloged at inferred fols. 1r–10v, and The Sege of Troye, at inferred fols. 11r–24v. Brie argues, and current scholarship confirms, that the two prose works abridge John Lydgate's Siege of Thebes and Troy Book respectively. A complete scan of Archiv volume 130 and a complete PMLA issue containing Nathaniel E. Griffin's independent 1907 Troy edition are cached locally. No complete public facsimile of MS. Rawl. D. 82 was found: Late Medieval English Scribes exposes selected images of fols. 1r and 25r, but only fol. 1r overlaps CME00076, while the Bodleian offers mediated scans and high-resolution photography by request. Any such request should cover the complete 34-leaf codex, six later endleaves, and binding surfaces, then map the corpus works separately to fols. 1r–24v.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: After 1422 and probably by the mid-fifteenth century; source edition published in 1913
catalogSummary.editor: Friedrich Brie; manuscript transcript by Fritz Jung
catalogSummary.region: England; the surviving copy's language is localized to Northamptonshire, not necessarily the place of composition
catalogSummary.title: The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye

catalogingSubject.label: The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The two distinct anonymous Middle English prose romances grouped together by Friedrich Brie's 1913 article and the CME00076 encoding. The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye have separate titles, narrative subjects, Lydgate source works, printed page runs, and manuscript loci, although Brie argues that one anonymous adapter composed both and the Bodleian records one hand copying them consecutively. This cataloged composite is distinct from John Lydgate's source poems, Bodleian MS. Rawl. D. 82 as a complete physical object, Fritz Jung's working transcript, Brie's German source study and edition, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in repository XML.

contentParts.label: The Sege of Thebes
contentParts.summary: Anonymous prose abridgment of Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, from Oedipus through the war of the Seven and Theseus's intervention.

contentParts.label: The Sege of Troye
contentParts.summary: Anonymous prose abridgment of Lydgate's Troy Book, from Jason's expedition through the final destruction of Troy.

assertion.notes: Brie's circa-1450 wording and the modern mid-fifteenth-century characterization are approximate. The normalized upper bound therefore uses the current catalog's broader fifteenth-century attestation rather than converting circa 1450 into an exact terminus.

dateStatements.display: After Lydgate's c. 1421–1422 Siege of Thebes and probably by about the middle of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The whole-composite summary uses 1422 because both works cannot predate the later of their two Lydgate sources. The normalized upper bound uses the broad fifteenth-century attestation rather than treating circa 1450 as exact.

dateStatements.display: After Lydgate completed Troy Book in 1420 and probably by about the middle of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: Late Medieval English Scribes narrows the copy to 1450–1500, while the current institutional catalog retains the broader fifteenth-century range.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth century in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. D. 82

extent.description: Two complete prose works represented by two DIV1 elements. The encoding has 23 printed-page breaks: Thebes on pp. 47–52 and 269–272, Troy on pp. 273–285. It records 45 manuscript-folio change milestones, 37 notes, and 758 italic expansion spans. The manuscript work locus is inferred fols. 1r–24v inside a 34-leaf codex with six later endleaves.

formStatements.description: Two continuous prose narratives. The removed or adjacent verse mentioned in XML revision history and the following Gower extract are outside this cataloging boundary.

genres.label: Classical romance
notes: The works recast legendary Theban and Trojan histories as late-medieval prose narratives of rulers, warfare, counsel, betrayal, and civic destruction.
The German editorial headnote and source-edition publication date are documented but do not make the medieval prose works German or composed in 1913.
The two prose works appear to survive continuously only in MS. Rawl. D. 82. This record does not turn Lydgate manuscripts into witnesses of the distinct prose adaptations.
Brie's claim to print previously unknown texts conflicts with Griffin's 1907 Troy edition; the conflict affects publication history, not the immediate source of CME00076.
The XML's manuscript folio milestones begin after opening recto text and mark transitions, so their count is not a count of all photographed or extant page surfaces.

A complete physical-source facsimile must include all of MS. Rawl. D. 82 rather than only fols. 1r–24v; the work mappings remain Thebes 1r–10v and Troy 11r–24v, both currently described by the Bodleian as inferred loci.

next_steps: Reassess Brie's stylistic argument against current linguistic and narratological scholarship.
Keep separate anonymous authorship records for each work even while retaining the supported shared-adapter hypothesis.
Do not treat one manuscript hand as proof of one author.
openQuestions.question: How securely can one anonymous adapter be assigned to both prose romances?

next_steps: Review LALME LP 6640 and later scholarship on the prose works' language.
Separate authorial dialect from the Rawl. D. 82 copy's Northamptonshire localization.
Reconcile Brie's mid-century witness estimate, the current broad fifteenth-century catalog date, and LMES's 1450–1500 copy date.
openQuestions.question: Can either prose adaptation be dated or localized more narrowly than after its Lydgate source and around the middle of the fifteenth century?

next_steps: Compare Griffin's Gallicism and Dares arguments with Brie's Lydgate parallels and later source studies.
Retain Lydgate's Troy Book as the confirmed source in discovery metadata while preserving Griffin's earlier theory as historiography rather than an upstream entity.
openQuestions.question: What precise evidence finally excludes or qualifies Griffin's proposed unidentified French intermediary for The Sege of Troye?

assertion.notes: This is a witness-language localization, not direct evidence that both prose adaptations were composed in Northamptonshire.

assertion.notes: Brie infers common authorship from poetic style and recurring elaboration. One copying hand does not by itself prove one adapter.

scope.description: Prose abridgment and adaptation of two Lydgate verse works
1913 source-edition representation, introduction, and Lydgate comparison notes
Working transcript of MS. Rawl. D. 82, inferred fols. 1r–24v, used by Brie

subjects.label: Thebes and the Seven against Thebes
Fratricide, civil war, and civic destruction

summaries.value: Two anonymous Middle English prose romances abridge John Lydgate's great verse narratives of Thebes and Troy. The first recounts Oedipus, the rival claims of Eteocles and Polynices, the Seven against Thebes, fratricidal war, and Theseus's final intervention. The second begins with Jason and the Golden Fleece, follows the causes and battles of the Trojan War, and ends with Troy's betrayal and destruction and a moral against treason. CME00076 presents Friedrich Brie's 1913 single-manuscript edition with its short German transcription headnote and selected Lydgate references, but omits his longer German introduction.

Thebes is founded under Amphioun's line. Oedipus unknowingly kills his father and marries his mother Jocasta; their sons Eteocles and Polynices agree to alternate the crown, but Eteocles refuses to yield. Polynices gathers the Argive princes, the brothers kill one another, and Theseus finally compels burial for the fallen and punishes the Theban tyrant Creon.

The Troy narrative opens with Jason's Golden Fleece expedition and the first Greek destruction of Troy, then follows Priam's rebuilding, Paris's abduction of Helen, the Greek expedition and siege, Hector's death, Trojan betrayal, the wooden horse, and the destruction of the city. Its closing emphasizes the ruin produced by treason and falseness.

notes: This whole-composite discovery title does not assert that the manuscript presents the two works under one collective medieval heading.
titles.supplied_by: Combined from the two manuscript rubrics and modernized only by joining them with 'and'
titles.value: The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye
Zwei mittelenglische Prosaromane: The Sege of Thebes und The Sege of Troy

notes: The current Bodleian catalog transcribes the opening rubric 'Here bigynnethe Sege of Thebes'; the XML and Brie print 'Here bigynneth þe Sege of Thebes.'

titles.value: In the tyme of the fulle worthi Josue, as the story reherceth by þe writing of þe noble clerke Boas
the which myscheues iournay and sege was iiije yere bifore Remus and Remulus bigane the fundacione of Rome

notes: Brie's article title normalizes the final word as Troy; the manuscript rubric and preferred part title use Troye.

titles.value: As the noble and worthi clerke Guydo writethe
And alwey the ende of euery tresone and falsenes to sorowe and myschef at the last. Amen.
